value.market_read: WTI Curve: WTI front-month (Aug26) settled at $70.07, with the prior session firming after the latest WPSR pointed to tighter prompt balances at Cushing. The reported draw drove WTI higher into settlement, reinforcing the front of the curve as physical demand outpaced near-term supply. The curve is backwardated, with the M1–M12 spread at +$3.34 (Aug26 $70.09 vs Jun27 $66.75), signaling the market is pricing genuine near-term supply tightness rather than a storage surplus. The smooth, monotonic decline from front to back keeps the structure cleanly backwardated, with prompt-month strength rewarding inventory holders to release barrels. Watch: Whether subsequent Cushing draws sustain the backwardation or a demand wobble flattens the front spreads. Brent Curve: Brent front-month (Aug26) settled at $73.37, supported alongside WTI on the post-WPSR tightening read but with a flatter prompt structure. The M1–M12 spread sits at +$1.93, materially shallower than WTI's, indicating the global waterborne market is pricing less acute prompt tightness than the US domestic balance. The curve is broadly backwardated but choppy — note the kink around Mar27 ($74.15) where geopolitical risk premium tied to Hormuz/Iran exposure distorts the deferred contracts above the smooth glide. This bump suggests the market is hedging supply-disruption tail risk in early 2027 deliveries rather than reflecting a clean fundamental signal. Watch: Whether the deferred-month bulge resolves lower or persists as Hormuz headline risk stays elevated. RBOB Gasoline Curve: RBOB front-month settled at $2.7832, with the prompt holding a summer-demand premium as driving season pulls barrels forward. The steep front-to-summer backwardation — front month roughly 36 cents over the autumn trough near $2.18 — reflects peak seasonal gasoline pull, with the M1–M12 spread at +$0.3993. The curve collapses through autumn then rebuilds into the back (Apr27 $2.3971) as the calendar prices the post-summer demand cliff and the subsequent spring RVP transition. This shape is textbook seasonal: tight prompt summer balances giving way to shoulder-season surplus. Watch: Whether refinery runs and import flows keep pace with summer draws or the prompt premium erodes as the season matures. ULSD Diesel Curve: ULSD front-month settled at $3.1019, firm on the WPSR read and supported by lean distillate balances carried over from the heating-demand season. The curve is cleanly and steeply backwardated, with the M1–M12 spread at +$0.4206 (front $3.1019 vs Jun27 $2.6813), the steepest distillate prompt signal in the complex and a clear flag of near-term tightness. Unlike gasoline, ULSD shows no deferred rebuild — the monotonic decline implies the market sees no structural surplus building out the curve. The diesel strength continues to anchor distillate cracks even as we move past peak heating into summer. Watch: Whether distillate restocking ahead of next winter or a freight/industrial demand softening reshapes the back end.
value.kpi_wti: WTI front-month (Aug26) settled at $70.07, firming in the prior session as the latest WPSR signaled tighter prompt Cushing balances.
value.kpi_brent: Brent front-month (Aug26) settled at $73.37, supported alongside WTI but with a notably flatter prompt curve reflecting a less acute global tightness signal.
value.kpi_rbob: RBOB front-month settled at $2.7832, carrying a steep summer-demand premium as peak driving-season pull tightens prompt balances.
value.kpi_ulsd: ULSD front-month settled at $3.1019, holding firm on lean distillate balances and posting the steepest backwardation in the complex.
value.kpi_bw: The Brent–WTI spread sits at a narrow $3.30, signaling weak US export arb economics — barrels are more likely to stay domestic, and re-widening to restore export competitiveness would require WTI to ease relative to Brent.
value.kpi_curve_regime: Both crude curves are backwardated, but WTI's steeper +$3.34 M1–M12 versus Brent's +$1.93 implies the US domestic prompt is tighter than the global waterborne market, pricing near-term supply tightness over any storage surplus.
